speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE] interview with Matilda Weisel Klein. When the Hungarians came in around Pesach, around Passover, you said.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How--

speaker: interviewee
text: Around Passover, they took us away.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, but when--

speaker: interviewee
text: They came in before.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before. And how long did--

speaker: interviewee
text: A few years before.

speaker: interviewer
text: A few years before.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And could you describe to us how you were taken to the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I have to start with the rabbi.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sure.

speaker: interviewee
text: The rabbi was taken away the last day of Pesach.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which rabbi?

speaker: interviewee
text: Goldberg.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he was from the--

speaker: interviewee
text: The only rabbi in Svalava.

speaker: interviewer
text: Svalava rabbi.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. Right. They just grabbed him, and they couldn't take with them no clothes, no food, nothing. And we didn't know where they grabbed him. We just hear the second day.

speaker: interviewer
text: Of-- of--

speaker: interviewee
text: That the rabbi was taken away with the rebbetzin and-- just the two of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm. By whom?

speaker: interviewee
text: By the Hungarian army.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then we had to get ready until the next day to go to-- to a working camp. They said, we didn't know where we are going, that all the Jews have to come to the schul. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And we have to pack as much as we can carry in one day. Is it important what we took with us? Yes. What did-- I would have asked you. What did you take?

speaker: interviewee
text: First, in one day, we had to make knapsacks from the covers-- bed covers. Every child had a knapsack, and every child had underwear, towel, and a few sheets. And that's about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: They-- who determined that? Your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your mother decided what you're taking.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you take any jewelry or things of that--

speaker: interviewee
text: We had to turn it in, the jewelry. We didn't have many jewelry. We didn't have much.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you take cooking equipment, pots, pans?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we did-- not much. And we were taken into the schul and slept overnight. And in the morning, the whole population was taken to the train station. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we went-- it was one-hour ride to Munkacs, and they put us into a brick factory. The brick factory was the name [? Kalush. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: Brick factory. [? MK: ?] [? Kalush. ?] The [? Kalush ?] brick factory.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. This was the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that's in Munkacs.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this in Munkacs.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you slept over in the schul, what did you sleep on?

speaker: interviewee
text: On the floor.

speaker: interviewer
text: On the floor. And did you have more than one schul in--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we did-- two.

speaker: interviewer
text: Svalava.

speaker: interviewee
text: Two.

speaker: interviewer
text: Two. And which schul was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: The newer building, the bigger one.

speaker: interviewer
text: The bigger building.

speaker: interviewee
text: The other was a [INAUDIBLE], and this was a-- the new-- I don't know how new it was, but it was a bigger building.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how many were you in there that night?

speaker: interviewee
text: The whole population.

speaker: interviewer
text: About 300 people or--

speaker: interviewee
text: More, because this was 300 families-- more.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. And who went in your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mother and three children, and I had an aunt, too, who went with us. And besides, we had cousins and aunts. We had a lot of relatives.

speaker: interviewer
text: You all went together?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you get to the schul?

speaker: interviewee
text: Walked.

speaker: interviewer
text: You walked. How far was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: One kilometer.

speaker: interviewer
text: And everybody walked. And were you-- were you being escorted by soldiers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, to the schul we walked on our own.

speaker: interviewer
text: On your own.

speaker: interviewee
text: But from the schul to the train, we were escorted by soldiers.

speaker: interviewer
text: German or Hungarian?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hungarian.

speaker: interviewer
text: Hungarian. The local population-- did they see you going to the schul and then to the station?

speaker: interviewee
text: Probably.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any-- do you remember any kind of comments that these people made?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, but they were happy they get rid of the Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, how did you know they were happy?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because they were--

speaker: interviewer
text: I mean, did they--

speaker: interviewee
text: They didn't like the Jews. They were happy to get our-- the houses, whatever is left in the house. They were grabbing it the minute we stepped out.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in the schul itself, your first-- you slept one night there.

speaker: interviewee
text: One night.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened? How did people behave there?

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't slept at all. They were scared. They were guessing where we are going, where we will stay, what will happen to us. And my mother was such a strong believer, she just said, God will help us. Don't worry. Just be calm. Nothing will happen to us. We will be all right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did a lot of people feel that way?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to those who didn't feel that way? Anybody try to escape?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Escape where?

speaker: interviewer
text: Where? I just remembered. We had a tenant, an old bachelor, non-Jewish. And he made the offer to my mother that he will take the two girls, and he'll hide them. Two girls being you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Me and my sister, to leave with him, and he will hide us. And mother says no. What will happen to me, happen to them. I know what an old bachelor wants to do with those girls.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm. So he remained in your house?

speaker: interviewee
text: He remained in the house. Yeah. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you went to the-- when you were marched to the train, what kind of train did you get into?

speaker: interviewee
text: Cattle train.

speaker: interviewer
text: Cattle-- the first-- to the brick factory in Munkacs?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: You also went-- You went by cattle train.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, but it was just a two-hour ride.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did everybody go in there, or were there--

speaker: interviewee
text: Like sheep to the slaughter. Like sheep to the slaughter.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you took all your things with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. OK. And in Munkacs in the [? Kalush ?] brick factory, what happened there? There were organizers. Our own people were organizers. They-- they had huts. And they put too many-- as many people as could get in. They have mattresses on the floor, and one close to the other. We even couldn't walk through. That's how we were. And then was a kitchen, and we got food from there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who ran the kitchen?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I-- I have a feeling it was the Jews from Munkacs. They were still in their houses, and they were sending it in.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm. By the way--

speaker: interviewee
text: The Jewish community did it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What date was that? Do you remember the date that you went to--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. It was April-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] around April 5th.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm. And was it still Pesach?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, a day after Pesach-- two days after Pesach.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that was in 1944.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was there a Judenrat there to run the ghetto? This was considered a ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was considered a ghetto. Yeah, it was organized. This was organized. But like I said, I was busy working in the ghetto. Because the Germans, they were already Germans. I didn't see them. They were running the ghetto. And they wanted to cut the hair for all the girls. Everybody should cut their hair. And some girls were very upset-- the crown of their head. One girl wanted to commit suicide, and she's not going to cut her hair.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were supposed to cut them yourself? Or they wanted to cut it?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know what their plan was, but then they negotiated with the Germans, and the Germans agreed that very short hair-- everybody can have short hair. And they set up a place, a big room. And I was with the hair cutters. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And besides hair-- I was cutting hair the whole day. Besides this, we had-- how you say this? At night, we had to stand [NON-ENGLISH]. You know what [NON-ENGLISH]? Guard?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. Changing-- our own people. So I had a few hours at night to stand.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did you have to-- whom were you guarding?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you being counted?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. But in front of every house-- it wasn't houses. It was just from bricks laid like huts. And in front of every house was supposed to stand a guard. And this was in shifts the whole night.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm. Who picked you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Our own people.

speaker: interviewer
text: But who were our own people--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I had to because just the young people were standing guard.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see. But who picked you to cut hair? Were you--

speaker: interviewee
text: I offered.

speaker: interviewer
text: You offered?

speaker: interviewee
text: I offered.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you get--

speaker: interviewee
text: And my brother did, too-- both of us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you get paid?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: No. Nobody paid you. And you didn't pay for food? The--

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Jewish community of Munkacs--

speaker: interviewee
text: Of Munkacs provided with food.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was-- what were they like in the Jewish community? Were they friendly towards you from Svalava?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. They were friendly.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a sense of, you know, [HEBREW]?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very much so. Very much so. The Jews always stick together. They-- no matter what town you came from, they stick together.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was-- did your mother do any cooking there? Or was there a community kitchen only?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: No cooking.

speaker: interviewee
text: No cooking.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were you all just your town in this area?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Just--

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there other people there, too?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, just us.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did people do after work or while you were standing guard? Was there any kind of relationship between the young people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. It was-- yeah. We had a little fun-- not much. We were singing a little bit, but not much. Everybody was scared of the future. So how much fun can you have if you don't know what tomorrow will happen to you? And we didn't have any news anymore-- no newspapers. So we were just guessing where we will be taken.

speaker: interviewer
text: You knew you would be taken somewhere.

speaker: interviewee
text: We knew that we'll be taken. And we were guessing at working camps. Somebody said to Germany. Somebody said to Poland. And we will go to working camps.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm. Did you have any information about what was happening in-- about concentration camps?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Or even the working camps?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: None.

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: If you didn't work cutting hair or something else, what did-- what other things could you do in that--

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] People were sitting around and doing nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any help for the kids, little kids? Did anybody organize--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah. They had for little kids-- they had kindergartens, nurseries. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Organized by the--

speaker: interviewee
text: But you have to know that a scared people can do nothing. They don't have the energy even to survive. If you're scared of the future, of your life, then you don't have the energy to do anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you in [? Kalush? ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: How long? Five weeks.

speaker: interviewer
text: Five weeks?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: By the way, [? Kalush, ?] is that the name of a person?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a person, and after him was named his factory.

speaker: interviewer
text: A Jewish person, or--?

speaker: interviewee
text: Probably, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you mentioned that your rabbi had been taken away. Did you ever find out what happened to your rabbi?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was with us in the [? Kalush ?] factory.

speaker: interviewer
text: You met up with him there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we did. This is a whole story about the rabbi. And nobody knows about that. So my mother wasn't very friendly with the rebbetzin before. I don't know why. I don't want to talk bad things about dead people, but she said that the rebbetzin is a snob. So she didn't-- but she wasn't a friend before with the rebbetzin. But in the [? Kalush ?] factory, they were always talking very quietly. Nobody should hear. They had always conversations. And what I hear from this conversation, the rebbetzin was complaining that her husband sits in a corner, depressed. He's not eating. He's not moving around. He's just fasting and praying. And she was very concerned about him. And she needed help, but she didn't want to go to nobody. And my mother find out when they were taken from the house, they didn't have anything with them. So I took over for them two sheets and two towels. But this was a big secret. Nobody's not supposed to know that the rabbi is that bad. And when we went into the cattle cars, the rabbi was in the same car. He was in a corner, sitting with his knees up to here. And they put a sheet in the corner to separate him.

speaker: interviewer
text: To separate him from everybody or from the women?

speaker: interviewee
text: From everybody.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did they do that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I don't know. So he got separated, but he still didn't eat, and he was very depressed. Maybe he was sick.

speaker: interviewer
text: So should I continue? You want-- Yes, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. When we arrived-- I told you the first day in Auschwitz, this was the most traumatic thing. And it has to do with the rabbi. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: When we went out of the cars, they took away the men. The men has to go separate, right? And the women and children were walking towards-- Mengele was standing with a stick, and he was just showing with the stick who goes to the right and who goes to the left.

speaker: interviewee
text: And Mother was holding onto both of us. And he just showed that I had to go this way with his stick. And I wanted to go with Mother. And he said no. And I was pushed away. Now, we had our aunt with us. She was 28 years old, and she had a baby before we were taken to Auschwitz. And the baby died. And this aunt thought that she needs special treatment. So she went to Mengele, and she told him, I just had a baby. I don't know if I can do some hard work. And he says to her, oh, yeah. She knew very good German. We'll send you to a sanatorium. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So-- and then, I was pushed away. I never saw Mother again. But I didn't know that. And we were walking, a few hundred girls, some-- a distance from two, three blocks. And we saw, not too far away, fire on the floor-- big fire. And men were holding bodies by the legs, some by the hands, made like this and into the fire. And we stopped and started to scream. It was such a shock. And we didn't move. And this picture is in front of my eyes as long as I live. And we didn't want to move. And then the girls started to scream, Go on! Go on! If you don't go on to the showers, you will be thrown into the fires too.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were the girls?

speaker: interviewee
text: Our girls who was there for a few years already.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, I see. Were they kapos? Or--

speaker: interviewee
text: You can say kapos. And they said that they are throwing into the fires dead people. They are dead. And then we went into the showers, OK? And in the showers, the brain doesn't work like it worked before. I had the feeling I died. And this is-- this is hell. And I live with this since then.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, let's go back to the rabbi. Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Later on-- we didn't believe that our parents are dead. They told us, 'You see the flames? They are all in the flames. They are all burned.' But we didn't believe it. We didn't take it in. The old people and the children went to the gas chambers, right? So my mother probably was with the rabbi and the rebbetzin.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: They had to undress and to go into the gas chambers. And they were gassed and burned, right? Guess so.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. My mother didn't have the dignity to die like a person. She had to undress in front of the rabbi. I will never, ever forgive the Germans for this. Because I was working in a munitions factory. I was making ammunition. And they didn't want to spend one bullet to kill my mother. She should die in dignity. Just to undress her in front of man-- and I will never, ever forgive this.

speaker: interviewer
text: You did not know this, though, at the time, did you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Later, I did.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened with the rabbi?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was burned.

speaker: interviewer
text: He was burned, too.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you find out about your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: A few months later.

speaker: interviewer
text: While you were in Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. When we left Auschwitz. Then I see there is no hope for them. Then it dawned on me that those fires are really burning people. And we had the smell. And we saw the fires from the chimneys. We saw the fire on the ground, burning people. And it-- the brain just didn't take it in, that such a thing can happen.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about your aunt? Did you ever see your aunt again?

speaker: interviewee
text: Never. She was burned. She went into the sanatorium that-- that he promised her.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you went into the showers, what happened there for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was in shock. They shaved, shaved all the hair. And they left us stand naked for a whole night.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, we came and-- oh, this, I have to tell. When we came in, it was night, in the evening. And it was tremendous lights, electric lights. And music was playing. And my mother says, look at that. We came to heaven. What bad things can happen to us? We have such lights and music. Who was playing the music?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know-- our people. The musicians was our girls. So, she went to heaven this night. But I didn't know it. I didn't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were marched first into the showers.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And before you took the showers, you were--

speaker: interviewee
text: Shaved.

speaker: interviewer
text: --all shaved. And what else happened to you there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was very much in shock.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were you with? Did you know anybody you were with?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, here is the problem. With a name like Weisel, everything went after the ABC, yeah? And the Weisel is the W. And I was the last one. And I was nobody I knew-- not relatives and not friends and not from our town. And all the way in the camps, I had nobody I knew, and this was terrible.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you with Hungarian Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So could you communicate, language-wise?

speaker: interviewee
text: I could. I could. I knew Polish, too, the communication, when they're Polish, because those girls who stayed a long time-- they were all Polish girls.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the relationship between the Polish girls and the new Hungarians?

speaker: interviewee
text: No good.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what way?

speaker: interviewee
text: They hated each other. The girls who were there a long time-- just the strong ones survived. They were four years in the camps. And they were the strong ones because the weak ones couldn't survive for four years.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did they became strong? Hitting other people, stealing, hitting, tough, rough. And they were rough. They couldn't survive-- I didn't see it then, but I see it now. They couldn't survive any other way, just to hit the other fellow Jew. Were you ever hit?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, many times.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any good kapos?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I had a-- in the working camp, I had a good kapo, yeah. Yeah-- one of our girls.

speaker: interviewer
text: Once you went through the shower, then what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: We stayed the whole night. They didn't have clothes for us. So we had to wait till the clothes goes through-- how you say?

speaker: interviewer
text: Disinfection.

speaker: interviewee
text: Disinfection, yeah. And then we get a gray dress, and that's about it-- no underwear. And with this gray dress, they took us to our block. They called it a block.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which block were you in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Don't remember. I think it's 12, but I'm not sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: What lager?

speaker: interviewee
text: A.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were in Lager A?

speaker: interviewee
text: A.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was where?

speaker: interviewee
text: Birkenau-Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Birkenau-Auschwitz. And how-- how large was the lager? The block?

speaker: interviewee
text: The block?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Around 1,500 people.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how many were in where you were staying?

speaker: interviewee
text: Around 1,500.

speaker: interviewer
text: All in one--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I'm not sure. I'm not sure. It was three-- the beds were-- beds-- hmm. How you call it?

speaker: interviewer
text: Bunks.

speaker: interviewee
text: Bunks were three stories. And in every-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Every bunk was 12 girls-- six here and six here. And the feet went like this. Here's six-- so OK, it was around 1,000 in one block.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you get a number there?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they give you a number on your uniform? Or did they tell you what your number was?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how did they do their [GERMAN]? Or did they have one?

speaker: interviewee
text: They did have [GERMAN]. They just counted the people. They take down the names, but that's it. And they counted twice a day, was the [GERMAN].

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you in that lager?

speaker: interviewee
text: Approximately three months.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you do there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. Sitting on the floor. Nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did people talk about?

speaker: interviewee
text: Food.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you make any relationships with any of the women?

speaker: interviewee
text: I did. I did. But it was superficial. I couldn't relate to-- and I was always looking for relatives or friends. Couldn't find any. Even when I find, they was far away in a different block, and just could see them for a while, and that's it. And I couldn't find anyone.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there incidents of people not being very nice to each other?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Amongst the-- the women in the block?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what way?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were fighting for a piece of bread. One was stealing from the other one a piece of bread, and they got in a fight. Yeah. I don't know if it's important. They said that the Germans put in the food something so the girls won't have their periods. But we were happy not to have it. Because we had the periods. We have no napkins. We had not a piece of paper. We didn't have panties, and the blood was just running down. And it dried, and we peeled it off. And when the periods is gone, it was a relief.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was relief.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there are any sexual relations between women there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did men ever come into the--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Well, yeah-- they came to clean the latrines.

speaker: interviewer
text: Jewish men.

speaker: interviewee
text: Jewish men.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a special name for people who came to clean the latrines?

speaker: interviewee
text: Scheiss Kommando.

speaker: interviewer
text: Scheiss Kommando?

speaker: interviewee
text: Commando.

speaker: interviewer
text: Scheiss Kommando.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know what 'scheiss' mean?

speaker: interviewer
text: No.

speaker: interviewee
text: No?

speaker: interviewer
text: What does it mean?

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] Um-- OK. It doesn't matter.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they clean the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Shit.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Did anybody get any special treatment in your--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who?

speaker: interviewee
text: The girls who helped the kapos to keep the people in discipline-- they got special treatment.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did that--

speaker: interviewee
text: Food.

speaker: interviewer
text: They got--

speaker: interviewee
text: Piece of bread.

speaker: interviewer
text: Piece of bread. And how did they do this?

speaker: interviewee
text: How did they do this? I have an incident I can tell you how. It's a-- but it happened not in the A lager-- in the B lager. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] One night, I hear, the middle of the night, a person came into the [GERMAN]. She was living in a little thing in the same block. And she said she needs 10 girls to take to another block to clean it up because a transport is just coming in in the morning, and she needs 10 girls to clean up this. And the girls who were cleaning up, they were called [GERMAN]. And those [GERMAN] had a special treatment, but they were working. They worked hard. They cleaned up the block. They brought the food from the kitchen, and they served the food. So those girls had food. And I told my friends, oh, we are going to be [GERMAN] now. And we jumped down off the beds. And the [GERMAN] came out, and she find us there. And she took us over to this block, clean it up. Because in a while, a transport-- a new transport is coming in with a few hundred girls.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm going to have to have this story on the next tape.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.
